cheatsheet:
Access Control
- open / public: Accessible from any source file in any module that imports the module.
- internal (default): Accessible from any source file within the same module.
- fileprivate: Accessible only within its own defining source file.
- private: Accessible only within the enclosing declaration.
- private(set): Read-only from the outside, but writeable from within the same scope.
description: Encapsulate a `Vault` using `private` and `private(set)`, and provide an explicit `internal` initializer.
hints: 'private' limits access to the current type, while 'private(set)' allows others to read but not write.
An 'internal' initializer is accessible throughout your module (the default level).
Explicitly write 'internal init(code: Int) { ... }' to practice the syntax.
Use 'self.code = code' to initialize the private property.
lesson:
Lesson: Access Control Basics
Access control restricts access to parts of your code from other source files and modules. This is essential for encapsulation.

Core Levels:
- 'private': Only accessible within the current type (struct, class, etc.).
- 'fileprivate': Accessible anywhere in the same file.
- 'internal': Accessible throughout the same module (default).
- 'private(set)': The property is readable from anywhere (based on its level), but only writeable from within the current scope.

Example:
    struct Account {
        private var balance: Int
        private(set) var id: String
        internal init(id: String, balance: Int) {
            self.id = id
            self.balance = balance
        }
    }

Best Practices:
- Default to 'private' and expose members only as needed.
- Use 'private(set)' for read-only state like IDs or status flags.
title: Access Control 1

cheatsheet:
Access Control
- open / public: Accessible from any source file in any module that imports the module.
- internal (default): Accessible from any source file within the same module.
- fileprivate: Accessible only within its own defining source file.
- private: Accessible only within the enclosing declaration.
- private(set): Read-only from the outside, but writeable from within the same scope.
description: Expose library components using `public` and `open` to control subclassing and access from other modules.
hints: 'public' means the type can be used, but not inherited from, outside the module.
'open' is only for classes; it allows both usage AND subclassing from other modules.
Mark your struct as 'public' and your class as 'open'.
Instantiate both and print their names as requested.
lesson:
Lesson: Public vs Open
When building frameworks or libraries, you use 'public' and 'open' to define how other modules interact with your code.

Key Differences:
1. 'public': Accessible from any module. You can use the type, call its methods, and access its properties. However, you cannot subclass a public class or override its methods outside the module where it's defined.
2. 'open': The most permissive level. Like public, but it *also* allows other modules to subclass the class and override its methods.

Note: 'open' only applies to classes and class members. Structs cannot be open because they don't support inheritance.

Example:
    public struct SharedData { ... }
    open class BaseService { ... }

Best Practices:
- Use 'public' by default for APIs you want to expose.
- Use 'open' only when you specifically intend for users to customize your class through inheritance.
title: Access Control 2

cheatsheet:
Error Handling
- Defining Errors: Use an enum that conforms to 'Error'.
- Throwing: Use 'throw MyError.something' inside a function marked 'throws'.
- Handling:
  - do { try function() } catch { ... }
  - try?: Returns an optional, nil if an error was thrown.
  - try!: Disables error propagation; crashes if an error is thrown.
- Rethrows: Used when a function takes a throwing closure and propagates its errors.
description: Implement a throwing function from a protocol, then catch and print the error message "Too cold" when the temperature is below 1000.
hints: The 'heat' function in your struct must include 'throws' to match the protocol.
Use 'throw FurnaceError.tooCold' when the level is less than 1000.
Wrap the call in a 'do { try ... } catch { ... }' block to handle the error.
Print "Too cold" in the catch block to satisfy the output requirement.
title: Error Integration

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Write a generic function `swapPair` that takes two different types and returns them in a swapped tuple.
hints: Generics let you work with placeholders like <A, B> in your function signature.
The signature should look like 'func swapPair<A, B>(_ first: A, _ second: B) -> (B, A)'.
Return a tuple with the second parameter first: '(second, first)'.
Call 'swapPair("Iron", 3)' to get the expected output of (3, "Iron").
lesson:
Lesson: Generic Functions
Generic functions use 'placeholder' type names (like T, U, A, B) in angle brackets after the function name. This lets the function work with any type while keeping it type-safe.

Core Concepts:
1. Placeholder Syntax: The name in brackets (e.g., <T>) is a placeholder that Swift replaces with a real type at compile time.
2. Reusability: You can write one function that works for many types instead of duplicating logic for Int, String, etc.

Example:
    func printTwice<T>(_ item: T) {
        print(item)
        print(item)
    }

Note: If a function uses multiple independent generic types, list them all in the brackets: <A, B>.
title: Generic Function

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Create a generic struct `Box<T>` that can store a value of any type.
hints: Use 'struct Box<T> { ... }' to define a generic type.
The value property should be of type 'T'.
When you call 'Box(value: 7)', Swift infers that 'T' is 'Int' automatically.
Print 'box.value' to satisfy the output check.
lesson:
Lesson: Generic Types
You can define your own generic structures, classes, and enumerations that work with any type, similar to Swift's 'Array' and 'Dictionary'.

Core Concepts:
1. Type Parameters: Placed in angle brackets (e.g., <T>) after the type name. This name is used as a placeholder for the real type.
2. Type Inference: Swift often infers the concrete type based on the values you pass to an initializer.
3. Reusable Models: Generic types allow you to build flexible data containers and models.

Example:
    struct Box<T> {
        var content: T
    }
    let intBox = Box(content: 5) // T is Int
    let strBox = Box(content: "Forge") // T is String

Note: Once initialized, a generic instance is locked into its concrete type (e.g., 'intBox' cannot suddenly store a String).
title: Generic Type

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Constrain a generic type parameter to the `Comparable` protocol to enable comparison operators like `>=`.
hints: By default, a generic T doesn't support comparison. Constrain it with 'T: Comparable'.
The 'Comparable' protocol allows the use of '<', '>', '<=', and '>=' operators.
Signature: 'func maxValue<T: Comparable>(_ first: T, _ second: T) -> T'.
Return the larger of the two values.
lesson:
Lesson: Type Constraints
Type constraints specify that a generic type parameter must inherit from a specific class or conform to a particular protocol.

Why Use Constraints?
1. Access to Functionality: Without constraints, you can only use properties and methods common to *all* types (like 'print'). By constraining to 'Comparable', you gain access to comparison operators.
2. Specialized Logic: You can write functions that only work for types that can be hashed, compared, or converted to a string.

Example:
    func findMax<T: Comparable>(_ a: T, _ b: T) -> T {
        return a > b ? a : b
    }

Note: You can use multiple constraints by listing them with commas or using 'where' clauses.
title: Type Constraints

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Define a protocol with an `associatedtype` to create generic placeholders for conforming types.
hints: 'associatedtype' is like a generic parameter, but it lives inside a protocol.
The conforming struct can specify what 'Item' is through type inference or an explicit 'typealias'.
Ensure you create an instance with two items (e.g., '["Iron", "Gold"]') to get the output '2'.
Print 'storage.items.count' to satisfy the check.
title: Associated Types

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Use a generic `where` clause to add specialized methods to an extension under specific protocol requirements.
hints: Use 'extension Array where Element: Equatable { ... }' to add the method.
The method 'allEqual()' should return a Bool.
Inside the function, you can use '==' because of the 'Equatable' constraint.
Call it on '[2, 2, 2]' to match the expected output.
title: Where Clauses

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
description: Add shared behavior to a protocol using a constrained extension (where `Self: Equatable`).
hints: Use 'extension Describable where Self: Equatable { ... }' to add functionality.
The 'Self' keyword refers to the type that conforms to the protocol.
Inside the extension, you can access the 'name' property defined in the protocol.
Create a Tool with the name "Anvil" to match the expected output.
title: Constrained Extension

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Add conditional conformance to a generic type, making it `Equatable` only when its elements are `Equatable`.
hints: Conditional conformance lives in an extension: 'extension Crate: Equatable where T: Equatable { }'.
Because 'T' is 'Equatable', Swift can automatically synthesize the '==' implementation for 'Crate'.
Compare two Crate instances with items [1, 2] to see 'true'.
This pattern avoids manual Equatable boilerplate for generic wrappers.
title: Conditional Conformance

description: Compose a small model layer using protocol-oriented design, combining requirements and default implementations.
hints: Define a protocol 'StatusProviding' with a 'status: String { get }' requirement.
Use a protocol extension to provide a default 'report()' method that prints the status.
Conform an 'Alloy' struct to 'StatusProviding'.
Create an instance with status "Alloy ready" and call its 'report()' method.
lesson:
Lesson: Protocol-Oriented Design
Swift is often described as a 'protocol-oriented' language. Instead of building deep class hierarchies, you build systems by composing small, focused protocols and providing default behavior through extensions.

Core Concepts:
1. Composition: Build complex types by conforming to multiple small protocols.
2. Behavior Injection: Use protocol extensions to 'inject' default methods into any conforming type.
3. Decoupling: Code against protocols rather than concrete types to make your system more flexible and testable.

Example:
    protocol Describable { var description: String { get } }
    extension Describable { func log() { print(description) } }

Note: This approach favors 'has-a' or 'acts-like-a' relationships over 'is-a' inheritance.
title: Model Layer Exercise

cheatsheet:
Memory Management (ARC)
- ARC: Automatic Reference Counting manages memory for class instances.
- Strong Reference: The default. Keeps the instance alive as long as the reference exists.
- Weak Reference: Does not keep the instance alive. Automatically becomes 'nil' when the instance is released. Must be 'var' and optional.
- Reference Cycle: Occurs when two instances hold strong references to each other, preventing them from ever being released.
- [weak self]: Used in closure capture lists to avoid reference cycles.
description: Prevent memory leaks by using `weak` references to break potential strong reference cycles.
hints: Use 'weak' for the reference that points back to the 'parent' or 'owner'.
Declare the reference in 'Operator' as 'weak var forge: Forge?'.
Because it's weak, it must be an optional and declared with 'var'.
Assign both 'forge.operatorRef' and 'operatorRef.forge' to create the cycle.
lesson:
Lesson: Memory Management & ARC
Swift uses Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) to manage memory for class instances. A reference cycle occurs when two instances hold strong references to each other, preventing their reference count from ever reaching zero.

Core Concepts:
1. Strong References: The default. Keeps an instance alive as long as the reference exists.
2. Weak References: Does not keep an instance alive. Automatically becomes 'nil' when the instance is released.
3. Breaking Cycles: Use 'weak' on one side of a reciprocal relationship (e.g., child-to-parent).

Example:
    class Person { var apartment: Apartment? }
    class Apartment { weak var tenant: Person? }

Note: You can use 'deinit' to print a message when an instance is deallocated, which is a powerful tool for verifying you've fixed a memory leak.
title: ARC Safety

cheatsheet:
Structs (Value Types)
- Definition: struct Name { var property: Type }
- Initialization: let instance = Name(property: value) (memberwise init)
- Access: Use dot notation: instance.property
- Mutability: Use 'var' for instances you want to modify
- Copying: Assigning a struct to a new variable creates a full, independent copy
- Methods: Methods that modify properties must be marked 'mutating'
description: Demonstrate value semantics by modifying an independent copy of a struct and verifying that the original remains unchanged.
hints: When you assign a struct to a new variable, a full copy is made.
Modifying the copy has no effect on the original because they occupy different memory.
Print both 'original.thickness' and 'copy.thickness' to see the difference.
lesson:
Lesson: Value vs. Reference Types
In Swift, structs are 'value types'. This means that when you assign a struct instance to a new variable, or pass it to a function, the entire value is copied.

Core Concepts:
1. Independence: Every variable has its own unique copy of the data. Changes to one copy never affect another.
2. Safety: Value semantics make it easier to reason about your code because you don't have to worry about hidden side effects from shared state.

Example:
    var a = Point(x: 0, y: 0)
    var b = a
    b.x = 10 // 'a' is still at (0, 0)

Note: This is in contrast to classes, which are 'reference types' where multiple variables can point to the same instance.
solution:
copy.thickness = 5
print(original.thickness)
print(copy.thickness)
title: Struct Copy Drill

cheatsheet:
Structs (Value Types)
- Definition: struct Name { var property: Type }
- Initialization: let instance = Name(property: value) (memberwise init)
- Access: Use dot notation: instance.property
- Mutability: Use 'var' for instances you want to modify
- Copying: Assigning a struct to a new variable creates a full, independent copy
- Methods: Methods that modify properties must be marked 'mutating'
description: Implement a `mutating` method in a struct to modify its internal state.
hints: Methods that modify struct properties must be marked with 'mutating'.
The 'increment' function should add 1 to the 'value' property.
Call 'increment()' twice on the instance to reach the value 3.
Ensure the instance is declared with 'var' so it can be mutated.
lesson:
Lesson: Mutating Methods
By default, the properties of a value type (like a struct) cannot be modified from within its instance methods. To modify the properties, you must opt in to 'mutating' behavior.

Core Concepts:
1. Intent: The 'mutating' keyword tells Swift that this method will change the instance's value.
2. Instance Mutability: You can only call mutating methods on instances declared as variables ('var').
3. Value Re-assignment: When a mutating method is called, Swift actually replaces the entire struct with a new one containing the updated values.

Example:
    struct LightSwitch {
        var isOn = false
        mutating func toggle() { isOn.toggle() }
    }

Note: Class methods do not need the 'mutating' keyword because classes are reference types and can always be mutated.
title: Mutating Counter

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
description: Implement a computed property with a getter and setter to convert between kilograms and pounds.
hints: The 'get' block should return the 'kg' value multiplied by 2.
The 'set' block should update 'kg' by dividing the 'newValue' by 2.
Swift provides 'newValue' automatically in the setter.
Setting 'pounds' to 10 should result in 'kg' becoming 5.
title: Computed Conversion

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
description: Use `willSet` and `didSet` to observe and react to changes in a property's value.
expectedOutput:
Will set to 3
Did set from 1
hints: 'willSet' is called just before the value changes; use 'newValue' to see the incoming value.
'didSet' is called just after the value changes; use 'oldValue' to see the previous value.
The format should be "Will set to \(newValue)" and "Did set from \(oldValue)".
Assigning the value 3 to 'pressure' will trigger both observers.
title: Observer Echo

cheatsheet:
Classes (Reference Types)
- Definition: class Name { var property: Type; init(...) { ... } }
- Reference Semantics: Multiple variables can point to the same instance
- Identity: Use === to check if two variables point to the same instance
- Initializers: Unlike structs, classes DO NOT get a memberwise initializer automatically
- Deinit: A deinitializer runs when the last reference to an instance is released
- Inheritance: Classes can inherit from other classes (subclassing)
description: Demonstrate reference semantics by modifying a class instance through a secondary reference and observing the change across all references.
hints: Classes are reference types; 'primary' and 'secondary' point to the exact same object in memory.
Modifying 'secondary.mode' will change the data that 'primary' is looking at.
This is a key difference from structs, which would have created an independent copy.
Print 'primary.mode' after the change to see the updated value.
lesson:
Lesson: Reference Semantics (Classes)
Classes are 'reference types'. When you assign a class instance to a new variable, you are not copying the data. Instead, you are creating a new reference to the *same* instance in memory.

Core Concepts:
1. Shared Instance: All variables pointing to the same class instance see the same data. If one variable changes a property, all other variables see that change.
2. Identity vs. Equality: Classes have identity. You can use the identity operator (===) to check if two variables point to the exact same object.
3. Mutation: Even if a class reference is declared with 'let', you can still modify its properties (if they are 'var'), because 'let' only prevents you from pointing the reference to a different instance.

Example:
    let a = Person(name: "Ada")
    let b = a
    b.name = "Bryan"
    print(a.name) // "Bryan"

Note: This shared behavior is powerful for managing state but requires careful coordination to avoid bugs.
solution:
secondary.mode = "Active"
print(primary.mode)
title: Class Shared State

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
description: Implement a `lazy` property that is only initialized the first time it is accessed.
hints: Lazy properties are created only when first read, saving memory and processing time.
Declare the property as 'lazy var title: String'.
Because lazy properties can change state, they must always be 'var'.
Use string interpolation: "Report \(id)".
lesson:
Lesson: Lazy Properties
A lazy stored property is a property whose initial value is not calculated until the first time it is used. This is useful for properties that are expensive to compute or depend on data not yet available at initialization.

Core Concepts:
1. Deferral: The property initializer only runs when the property is accessed.
2. var Requirement: You must always declare a lazy property as a variable ('var') because its value is retrieved after instance initialization completes.
3. Single Execution: The initializer runs only once; subsequent accesses return the same value.

Example:
    struct DataManager {
        lazy var importer = DataImporter()
        var data: [String] = []
    }

Note: Lazy properties are not thread-safe by default.
title: Lazy Builder

description: Provide a default implementation for a protocol method using an extension to share behavior across conforming types.
hints: Use 'extension Named { ... }' to provide a default method 'label()'.
Because of the default implementation, any struct that conforms to 'Named' gets 'label()' for free.
The method should return "Tool: \(name)".
Print the label of a Hammer to match the expected output.
lesson:
Lesson: Default Protocol Methods
Protocol extensions allow you to provide a default implementation for any method or property requirement of that protocol. This is a core pillar of Protocol-Oriented Programming.

Core Concepts:
1. Shared Behavior: Avoid duplicating code in multiple structs by defining it once in a protocol extension.
2. Optional Overriding: Conforming types can still provide their own implementation if the default behavior doesn't suit them.
3. Requirement Satisfaction: Providing a default implementation satisfies the protocol's requirement for all conforming types.

Example:
    protocol Greeter { var name: String { get } }
    extension Greeter { func greet() { print("Hi, \(name)") } }
    struct User: Greeter { let name: String }
    User(name: "Ada").greet() // "Hi, Ada"

Note: This allows protocols to act like 'mixins' or 'traits'.
title: Default Label

description: Use a protocol as a function parameter type to enable polymorphism and abstraction.
hints: The parameter 'source' in your function should be of type 'HeatSource', not 'Furnace'.
Because the function accepts the protocol, it can work with *any* type that conforms to 'HeatSource'.
Call the function with 'Furnace(heat: 1500)' to match the output check.
Abstraction allows you to swap out implementations without changing the calling code.
lesson:
Lesson: Protocols as Types
You can use a protocol as a type in places where you would normally use a concrete struct or class, such as in variable declarations or function parameters. This allows for polymorphic behavior.

Core Concepts:
1. Abstraction: The function doesn't care *which* specific type it receives, as long as it has the required behaviors (methods/properties).
2. Interchangeability: You can pass a 'Furnace', a 'Burner', or any other conforming type into the same function.
3. Type Access: Inside the function, you can only access members defined in the protocol.

Example:
    protocol Drawable { func draw() }
    func render(item: Drawable) {
        item.draw()
    }

Note: This is how Swift handles shared interfaces without needing class inheritance.
title: Protocol Parameter

description: Require multiple protocols for a single parameter using protocol composition (`&`).
hints: Use the '&' operator to require conformance to both protocols: 'Fueling & Venting'.
This composition is useful when you need multiple independent capabilities without defining a new inherited protocol.
Inside the function, you can access properties from both protocols on the 'vent' parameter.
Call the function with 'Vent(fuel: 2, airflow: 3)' to match the expected output.
lesson:
Lesson: Protocol Composition
Protocol composition allows you to require a type to conform to multiple protocols simultaneously. You do this using the ampersand (&) operator between the protocol names.

Core Concepts:
1. Ad-hoc Requirements: Composition is perfect for functions that need several independent behaviors (e.g., 'Encodable & Decodable').
2. Avoiding Bloat: You don't have to create a new protocol like 'FuelingAndVenting' just to combine two existing ones.
3. Flexibility: It allows you to keep protocols small and focused (the 'Interface Segregation Principle').

Example:
    func process(item: Named & Codable) {
        print("Processing \(item.name)")
    }

Note: You can combine more than two protocols by using multiple & symbols.
title: Composition Drill

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
description: Extend the built-in `Int` type with a `squared()` method to demonstrate how extensions can add functionality to existing types.
hints: You can add methods to any type, including Swift's built-in types like 'Int'.
Use 'extension Int { ... }' to add your method.
Inside the extension, 'self' refers to the specific integer value the method was called on.
The 'squared()' method should return 'self * self'.
lesson:
Lesson: Extending Existing Types
Extensions in Swift allow you to add new functionality to a type even if you don't have access to its original source code. This is often called 'retroactive modeling'.

Core Concepts:
1. Built-in Types: You can extend 'Int', 'String', 'Array', and other standard library types to add helper methods that suit your project.
2. Global Availability: Once an extension is defined, the new method is available on every instance of that type throughout your module.
3. self: Inside an extension, 'self' refers to the instance the extension is acting upon.

Example:
    extension String {
        func shouted() -> String { return self.uppercased() + "!" }
    }
    print("hello".shouted()) // "HELLO!"

Note: This is a powerful way to keep your call sites clean and expressive.
title: Extension Helper

cheatsheet:
Access Control
- open / public: Accessible from any source file in any module that imports the module.
- internal (default): Accessible from any source file within the same module.
- fileprivate: Accessible only within its own defining source file.
- private: Accessible only within the enclosing declaration.
- private(set): Read-only from the outside, but writeable from within the same scope.
description: Encapsulate sensitive data using `private` storage while providing a safe, public view through a computed property.
hints: Mark the 'code' property as 'private' to hide it from outside users.
Provide a public 'masked' computed property that returns a placeholder string.
The 'init' method is needed to set the private 'code' when an instance is created.
This pattern allows you to control exactly how data is exposed or hidden.
title: Access Wrapper

cheatsheet:
Error Handling
- Defining Errors: Use an enum that conforms to 'Error'.
- Throwing: Use 'throw MyError.something' inside a function marked 'throws'.
- Handling:
  - do { try function() } catch { ... }
  - try?: Returns an optional, nil if an error was thrown.
  - try!: Disables error propagation; crashes if an error is thrown.
- Rethrows: Used when a function takes a throwing closure and propagates its errors.
description: Define a custom error using an enum and implement a throwing function that triggers it under specific conditions.
hints: Custom errors are often defined as enums that conform to the 'Error' protocol.
The 'start' function should be marked with 'throws'.
Inside the function, use 'throw ForgeError.jam' when fuel is 0.
Call the function with fuel 0 and print "Jam" in the catch block.
lesson:
Lesson: Custom Errors with Enums
In Swift, it's common practice to define your own error types using an enum. This allows you to group related error cases together and provide clear, readable error handling throughout your code.

Core Concepts:
1. Error Protocol: To act as an error, your enum must conform to the built-in 'Error' protocol.
2. Throwing: Use the 'throw' keyword followed by an enum case to signal that an error has occurred.
3. Case Matching: You can catch specific enum cases to handle different errors in different ways.

Example:
    enum PrinterError: Error { case outOfPaper, noInk }
    func printDoc() throws { throw PrinterError.outOfPaper }

Note: Enums are ideal for errors because they are lightweight and can even carry additional data (associated values) about the error.
title: Error Route

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Create a generic struct `Box<T>` to store a value of any type and demonstrate type inference during instantiation.
hints: Use 'struct Box<T> { ... }' to define a generic type with placeholder T.
The value property should be of type 'T'.
When you call 'Box(value: "Iron")', Swift infers that 'T' is 'String'.
Print the value to match the expected output.
lesson:
Lesson: Generic Containers
Generic types allow you to build flexible containers that can store data of any type while maintaining strict type safety. This is how Swift's 'Array' and 'Optional' work under the hood.

Core Concepts:
1. Reusability: Write one data structure that works for all types.
2. Type Inference: Swift usually determines the concrete type of T based on the value you pass to the initializer.
3. Safety: Once an instance is created with a specific type (like String), it cannot store values of a different type.

Example:
    struct Box<T> { let content: T }
    let b = Box(content: 42) // T is Int

Note: Generic containers are the backbone of modular, reusable Swift code.
title: Generic Box

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Write a generic function that finds the minimum of two values by constraining the type parameter to the `Comparable` protocol.
hints: By default, Swift doesn't know if a generic 'T' can be compared. You must use 'T: Comparable'.
The 'Comparable' protocol enables the use of the '<' and '>' operators.
The function signature should be 'func minValue<T: Comparable>(_ first: T, _ second: T) -> T'.
Return the smaller of the two integers 3 and 5.
lesson:
Lesson: Protocol Constraints
Type constraints specify that a type parameter must conform to a particular protocol or inherit from a specific class. This gives your generic code access to the methods and properties defined by that protocol.

Core Concepts:
1. Functional Access: To use operators like '<' or '==', you must constrain your generic type to 'Comparable' or 'Equatable'.
2. Type Safety: Constraints ensure that your function only accepts types that actually support the logic you're writing.
3. Multi-Protocol Constraints: You can require multiple protocols using '&' (e.g., '<T: Comparable & Hashable>').

Example:
    func findMin<T: Comparable>(_ a: T, _ b: T) -> T {
        return a < b ? a : b
    }

Note: Most standard library types like Int, String, and Double already conform to 'Comparable'.
title: Comparable Min

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Define a protocol with an `associatedtype` and implement a generic stack that conforms to it.
hints: An 'associatedtype' is a placeholder for a type used inside a protocol.
The conforming struct must satisfy the 'mutating func push(_ item: Item)' requirement.
Because the struct is generic (Stack<T>), the 'Item' will be inferred as 'T'.
Pushing an element into an empty stack results in a count of 1.
lesson:
Lesson: Associated Types in Protocols
When defining a protocol, it's sometimes useful to declare a placeholder for a type without knowing what that type will be. An associated type gives a placeholder name to a type that is used as part of the protocol.

Core Concepts:
1. Late Binding: The actual type isn't specified until the protocol is adopted by a struct, class, or enum.
2. associatedtype Keyword: Used inside the protocol to declare the placeholder (e.g., 'associatedtype Element').
3. Type Requirements: You can even add constraints to associated types (e.g., 'associatedtype Element: Equatable').

Example:
    protocol Container {
        associatedtype Item
        var count: Int { get }
    }

Note: This is how protocols like 'Sequence' and 'Collection' work in the Swift Standard Library.
title: Associated Storage

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Extend a generic collection using a `where` clause to add specialized methods that are only available when certain protocol requirements are met.
hints: Use 'extension Array where Element: Equatable { ... }' to add the method.
The 'where' clause ensures the method is only available when the array elements can be compared.
Inside the function, use 'allSatisfy' or a loop to check if all elements match the first one.
Calling it on [1, 1, 2] should return false because not all elements are the same.
lesson:
Lesson: Conditional Extensions
You can use a 'where' clause in an extension to add methods and properties that are only available to instances of a generic type that satisfy specific requirements. This is common for adding specialized helpers to collections like 'Array'.

Core Concepts:
1. Targeted Helpers: Instead of adding a method to *every* array, you only add it to arrays of numbers, or arrays of equatable objects.
2. where Clause Syntax: Placed after the type name in the extension declaration (e.g., 'extension Array where Element: Comparable').
3. Logic Access: Inside the extension, you gain access to all the requirements of the constrained protocol (like '==' or '<').

Example:
    extension Array where Element: Numeric {
        func sum() -> Element { return reduce(0, +) }
    }

Note: This keeps your types lean by only providing functionality where it's relevant.
title: Where Clause

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Make a generic wrapper type conform to `Equatable` conditionally, allowing comparison only when the wrapped data is also `Equatable`.
hints: Conditional conformance means a type only adopts a protocol if its generic parts also adopt it.
The syntax is 'extension Wrapper: Equatable where T: Equatable { }'.
Because 'T' is Equatable, Swift can automatically synthesize the '==' operator for the 'Wrapper' struct.
Compare two instances containing the string "A" to verify they are equal.
lesson:
Lesson: Conditional Conformance
You can extend a generic type to conform to a protocol only under specific conditions—usually when its type parameter also conforms to that protocol. This is common for containers like 'Array' and 'Optional'.

Core Concepts:
1. Conformance Propagation: Allows protocols like 'Equatable' to work through generic wrappers.
2. Automatic Synthesis: If a struct's members are all Equatable, Swift can often generate the '==' implementation for you once you declare the conformance.
3. syntax: Use an extension with a 'where' clause: 'extension Box: Equatable where T: Equatable'.

Example:
    struct Box<T> { let value: T }
    extension Box: Equatable where T: Equatable { }
    // Now Box(value: 5) == Box(value: 5) works!

Note: This pattern is a key part of making generic types feel like 'first-class citizens' in Swift.
title: Conditional Conformance

cheatsheet:
Memory Management (ARC)
- ARC: Automatic Reference Counting manages memory for class instances.
- Strong Reference: The default. Keeps the instance alive as long as the reference exists.
- Weak Reference: Does not keep the instance alive. Automatically becomes 'nil' when the instance is released. Must be 'var' and optional.
- Reference Cycle: Occurs when two instances hold strong references to each other, preventing them from ever being released.
- [weak self]: Used in closure capture lists to avoid reference cycles.
description: Prevent reference cycles in closures by using a weak capture list (`[weak self]`) and safely unwrapping the optional instance.
hints: Use '[weak self] in' at the start of your closure to prevent it from holding a strong reference to the Logger.
Inside the closure, 'self' becomes an optional because it could be deallocated at any time.
Use 'if let self = self' or 'guard let self = self' to safely access properties like 'prefix'.
Print the prefix to satisfy the output requirement.
lesson:
Lesson: Closure Capture Lists
Closures can capture and store references to variables from the surrounding context. If a closure captures 'self' (a class instance) and 'self' also stores that closure in a property, a strong reference cycle is created, leading to a memory leak.

Core Concepts:
1. Capture List: A list of variables to be captured, placed in square brackets before the closure's parameters (e.g., '{ [weak self] in ... }').
2. Weak Capture: Capturing 'self' as 'weak' prevents the closure from keeping the instance alive. This breaks the reference cycle.
3. Optionality: A weakly captured reference is always an optional because the object it refers to could be deallocated while the closure still exists.

Example:
    func setup() {
        provider.onUpdate = { [weak self] in
            self?.refresh()
        }
    }

Note: Always use 'weak' capture when a closure has a long lifetime and refers back to its owner.
title: Weak Capture

cheatsheet:
Access Control
- open / public: Accessible from any source file in any module that imports the module.
- internal (default): Accessible from any source file within the same module.
- fileprivate: Accessible only within its own defining source file.
- private: Accessible only within the enclosing declaration.
- private(set): Read-only from the outside, but writeable from within the same scope.
description: Practice using varied access levels, including `fileprivate` and `private(set)`, to control visibility within a single source file.
hints: 'fileprivate' allows access from any code within the same source file, even outside the type's definition.
'internal' is the default and allows access from anywhere in the same module.
'private(set)' means the property is publicly readable but can only be changed by the type itself.
Print 'owner' and 'count' to match the expected output.
lesson:
Lesson: File-Scope Visibility
Swift provides 'fileprivate' to allow access to a member from any code within the same source file, but not from other files. This is particularly useful when you want to share data between a class and its extensions without exposing that data to the rest of the project.

Core Concepts:
1. private vs fileprivate: 'private' is restricted to the enclosing declaration (the brackets). 'fileprivate' expands that access to the entire .swift file.
2. Modular Hiding: Use 'fileprivate' for implementation details that multiple types in the same file need to share, but which should remain hidden from the outside world.
3. Complementary Levels: You can mix and match these levels (e.g., a 'fileprivate' struct with 'private' properties) to create fine-grained access rules.

Example:
    struct Vault {
        fileprivate var secret = "1234"
    }
    extension Vault {
        func reveal() { print(secret) } // Works because it's in the same file
    }

Note: 'fileprivate' is less common now that extensions can access 'private' members of the type they extend, but it remains useful for sharing between different types in the same file.
title: Access Control Nuance

description: Add a specialized method to a protocol using a constrained extension that requires conforming types to also implement `CustomStringConvertible`.
hints: Use 'extension Taggable where Self: CustomStringConvertible { ... }' to add the method.
The 'Self' keyword refers to the concrete type that is conforming to 'Taggable'.
Because of the constraint, your 'describeTag' method can access properties from both protocols.
Create a Tool with the tag "Forge" to match the expected output.
title: Protocol Extension Constraint

cheatsheet:
Structs (Value Types)
- Definition: struct Name { var property: Type }
- Initialization: let instance = Name(property: value) (memberwise init)
- Access: Use dot notation: instance.property
- Mutability: Use 'var' for instances you want to modify
- Copying: Assigning a struct to a new variable creates a full, independent copy
- Methods: Methods that modify properties must be marked 'mutating'
description: Utilize a struct's automatically generated memberwise initializer to create an instance with specific values.
hints: Swift provides a memberwise initializer for any struct that doesn't define its own custom init.
The initializer uses the property names as labels (e.g., 'metal:' and 'weight:').
Create an Ingot with metal 'Iron' and weight 3.
Print both properties separated by a space to match the output.
lesson:
Lesson: Memberwise Initializers
One of the most useful features of structs is the automatically generated 'memberwise initializer'. If you don't define any custom initializers for a struct, Swift provides one that lets you set all of its properties by name.

Core Concepts:
1. Automatic Generation: You don't have to write 'init(...)'. Swift creates it for you based on the properties you've declared.
2. Property Labels: The labels in the initializer match the property names exactly.
3. Default Values: If a property has a default value, it still appears in the memberwise initializer.

Example:
    struct Point { var x: Int; var y: Int }
    let p = Point(x: 10, y: 20) // No init code written!

Note: If you define even one custom initializer, you lose the automatic memberwise one (unless you define the custom one in an extension).
solution:
let ingot = Ingot(metal: "Iron", weight: 3)
print("\(ingot.metal) \(ingot.weight)")
title: Memberwise Init

cheatsheet:
Classes (Reference Types)
- Definition: class Name { var property: Type; init(...) { ... } }
- Reference Semantics: Multiple variables can point to the same instance
- Identity: Use === to check if two variables point to the same instance
- Initializers: Unlike structs, classes DO NOT get a memberwise initializer automatically
- Deinit: A deinitializer runs when the last reference to an instance is released
- Inheritance: Classes can inherit from other classes (subclassing)
description: Use the identity operator (`===`) to determine if two variables refer to the exact same class instance.
hints: The '===' operator checks if two references point to the exact same object in memory.
This is called 'identity', and it is only available for reference types (classes).
Since 'secondary' was assigned the value of 'primary', they both point to the same Forge instance.
Print the result of 'primary === secondary'.
lesson:
Lesson: Class Identity
Classes are reference types, which means multiple constants or variables can refer to a single instance of a class behind the scenes. Swift provides an identity operator to check for this condition.

Core Concepts:
1. Identical To (===): Returns true if two constants or variables refer to the exact same class instance.
2. Not Identical To (!==): Returns true if they refer to different instances.
3. Identity vs Equality: 'Identity' (===) means 'same instance in memory'. 'Equality' (==) means 'two different instances that happen to have the same values'.

Example:
    let a = Forge()
    let b = a
    print(a === b) // true
    let c = Forge()
    print(a === c) // false, even if their properties are identical

Note: Structs do not have identity because they are value types and are always copied.
solution: print(primary === secondary)
title: Class Identity

cheatsheet:
Properties
- Stored Properties: Hold values directly.
- Computed Properties: Do not store a value; instead, they provide a getter and an optional setter to calculate other properties indirectly.
- Property Observers: 'willSet' (runs before) and 'didSet' (runs after) a property changes.
- Lazy Properties: Initialized only when first accessed; must be 'var'.
- Static Properties: Shared across all instances of the type.
description: Implement a read-only computed property using shorthand syntax (omitting the `get` keyword).
hints: A read-only computed property only provides a getter.
Swift allows you to omit the 'get { ... }' braces and keyword for read-only properties, providing just the logic body.
Calculate 'bar' by dividing 'psi' by 14.
Print the value of 'gauge.bar' to match the output '2'.
title: Readonly Property

description: Provide a default implementation for a protocol method using an extension to reduce code duplication in conforming types.
hints: Protocol extensions allow you to provide code for methods defined in the protocol.
The extension should be 'extension Describable { ... }'.
Any struct that conforms to 'Describable' will automatically have the 'describe()' method.
Create a Tool with name "Anvil" and print its description to match the expected output.
lesson:
Lesson: Default Protocol Implementations
You can use protocol extensions to provide default implementations for any method or property requirements of that protocol. Conforming types can then use these implementations without having to provide their own.

Core Concepts:
1. Shared Behavior: Write once in an extension, use in many structs or classes.
2. Optional Overriding: Conforming types can still provide their own implementation if they need to customize the behavior.
3. Protocol-Oriented: This is a core part of Swift's design, favoring composition and shared behavior over deep inheritance.

Example:
    protocol Greeter { var name: String { get } }
    extension Greeter { func greet() { print("Hello, \(name)") } }

Note: Providing a default implementation effectively makes that requirement optional for conforming types.
title: Protocol Default

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
description: Add a static factory method to a struct using an extension to provide a standard configuration.
hints: Static methods are called on the type itself (e.g., 'Alloy.standard()').
The method should be defined as 'static func standard() -> Alloy'.
Inside the method, return a new Alloy instance with the name "Steel".
Print the name of the standard alloy to match the expected output.
title: Extension Static

cheatsheet:
Access Control
- open / public: Accessible from any source file in any module that imports the module.
- internal (default): Accessible from any source file within the same module.
- fileprivate: Accessible only within its own defining source file.
- private: Accessible only within the enclosing declaration.
- private(set): Read-only from the outside, but writeable from within the same scope.
description: Use `private(set)` to create a property that is publicly readable but can only be modified from within its own type.
hints: 'private(set)' allows you to set the access level for reading independently from writing.
The 'value' property is readable from anywhere, but only the 'Counter' struct can update it via 'increment()'.
This pattern ensures your internal state remains consistent by preventing outside callers from setting invalid values.
Call increment twice and print the value.
lesson:
Lesson: Private Setters
Sometimes you want a property's value to be visible to the outside world, but you only want it to be modified by the internal logic of its class or struct. Swift allows you to specify a more restrictive access level for a property's setter than for its getter.

Core Concepts:
1. private(set): The most common use case. It makes the property 'read-only' to the outside world but 'read-write' internally.
2. Encapsulation: This ensures that only your type's methods can change the state, preventing bugs from external code accidentally modifying data.
3. Consistency: By controlling the setter, you can guarantee that every update to the property goes through your approved logic (e.g., adding validation).

Example:
    struct Account {
        private(set) var balance: Int = 0
        mutating func deposit(_ amount: Int) { balance += amount }
    }

Note: You can use other levels too, like 'internal(set)' or 'fileprivate(set)'.
solution:
var counter = Counter()
counter.increment()
counter.increment()
print(counter.value)
title: Private(set)

cheatsheet:
Error Handling
- Defining Errors: Use an enum that conforms to 'Error'.
- Throwing: Use 'throw MyError.something' inside a function marked 'throws'.
- Handling:
  - do { try function() } catch { ... }
  - try?: Returns an optional, nil if an error was thrown.
  - try!: Disables error propagation; crashes if an error is thrown.
- Rethrows: Used when a function takes a throwing closure and propagates its errors.
description: Catch a specific enum error case using a specialized `catch` clause to handle different failure modes uniquely.
hints: Instead of a generic 'catch', use 'catch FuseError.blown' to target that specific error.
You can stack multiple catch blocks to handle different cases (blown, missing, etc.) separately.
The code inside the specific catch block will only run if the thrown error matches that case.
Print "Blown" when catching the 'blown' error.
title: Error Specific

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Create a generic struct `Pair<A, B>` that can store two values of potentially different types.
hints: Generics can take multiple placeholders inside the angle brackets (e.g., <A, B>).
Assign the first parameter to a property 'first' and the second to 'second'.
When you create 'Pair(first: "Iron", second: 3)', Swift infers A as String and B as Int.
Print both properties separated by a space.
lesson:
Lesson: Multiple Type Parameters
You can define generic types and functions that use more than one type parameter. Each parameter is given a unique placeholder name and separated by a comma inside the angle brackets.

Core Concepts:
1. Independent Types: Using <A, B> allows the two properties to be the same type or completely different types.
2. Descriptive Names: While A and B are common, you can use more descriptive names like <Key, Value> if they provide better context.
3. Positional Inference: Swift matches the types based on the order they appear in the declaration.

Example:
    struct Pair<T, U> {
        let first: T
        let second: U
    }
    let p = Pair(first: 1, second: "One") // T is Int, U is String

Note: This is how Swift's 'Dictionary' works, using <Key, Value> where Key must be Hashable.
title: Generic Pair

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Define a protocol with an `associatedtype` to create a flexible interface that allows conforming types to specify their own underlying data types.
hints: 'associatedtype' acts as a generic placeholder within a protocol definition.
The conforming type ('Bin') determines the concrete type of 'Item' by the data it provides.
In your struct, simply declare 'let item: String' to satisfy the protocol through type inference.
Print 'bin.item' to verify the result.
lesson:
Lesson: Associated Types
When you want a protocol to be generic, you use the 'associatedtype' keyword. This defines a placeholder name for a type that is used as part of the protocol's requirements.

Core Concepts:
1. Abstract Requirements: Protocols can define behavior (like 'storing an item') without knowing the exact type of that item.
2. Conformance Flexibilty: One struct can conform using 'Int', while another uses 'String', both satisfying the same protocol.
3. Type Inference: Conforming types often don't need to explicitly name the type; Swift infers it from the properties or methods implemented.

Example:
    protocol Container {
        associatedtype Element
        var count: Int { get }
    }

Note: This is a powerful way to build abstract, high-level interfaces that remain strictly type-safe.
solution:
let bin = Bin(item: "Iron")
print(bin.item)
title: Protocol Associated

description: Use the `any` keyword to create an existential type, allowing a variable to store any concrete instance that conforms to a protocol.
hints: In modern Swift, you must use the 'any' keyword when a protocol is used as a concrete type.
This creates an 'existential type', which is a container that can hold any type conforming to the protocol.
The variable 'source' should be typed as 'any HeatSource'.
Assign a Burner with heat 1500 and print the heat value.
lesson:
Lesson: Existential Types (any)
When you use a protocol as a type (for example, as a variable type or a function parameter), you are using an 'existential type'. In modern Swift, these must be explicitly marked with the 'any' keyword.

Core Concepts:
1. Type Erasure: The specific concrete type (like 'Burner') is 'erased', and the compiler only knows that the value satisfies the protocol requirements.
2. any Keyword: Signals that the variable can hold *any* conforming type and that there is a small performance cost for this flexibility.
3. Abstraction: Allows you to work with a heterogeneous collection of objects as if they were all the same type.

Example:
    var item: any Named = User(name: "Ada")
    item = Company(name: "Forge") // Both conform to Named

Note: Use 'any' when you need the flexibility to store different concrete types in the same variable.
solution:
let source: any HeatSource = Burner(heat: 1500)
print(source.heat)
title: Protocol Erasure

description: Combine multiple protocols using composition and store the resulting instance in an existential type marked with `any`.
hints: You can combine protocols with '&' and then wrap the whole thing in 'any'.
The variable type should be 'any Fueling & Venting'.
This creates an existential that guarantees the object implements both sets of requirements.
Instantiate a Vent(fuel: 2, airflow: 3) and print its properties.
lesson:
Lesson: Existentials with Composition
You can use the 'any' keyword with protocol composition to create a single type that requires multiple protocols. This is useful when you need a variable to hold an object that satisfies several independent behaviors.

Core Concepts:
1. syntax: Combine protocols with '&' and prefix with 'any' (e.g., 'any Named & Codable').
2. Unified Interface: The resulting type has access to all methods and properties from every protocol in the composition.
3. Flexibility: You can store any concrete instance that conforms to all the required protocols.

Example:
    var worker: any Runnable & Identifiable = Robot(id: 1)
    worker = Human(id: 2) // Both conform to both protocols

Note: This avoids the need to define 'mega-protocols' just to group common requirements together.
title: Protocol Composition Any

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Add a specialized method to a generic struct by applying a `where` clause directly to the method definition.
hints: Instead of constraining the entire struct, you can constrain just a single method using 'where'.
The method signature should be 'func allEqual() -> Bool where T: Equatable'.
This method will be available on all 'Store' instances, but can only be called if 'T' conforms to 'Equatable'.
Inside the method, use 'allSatisfy' to compare items.
lesson:
Lesson: Method-Level Constraints
You can apply constraints to individual methods within a generic type, rather than constraining the entire type. This allows a generic struct to have some methods that work for all types, and other methods that only work when specific conditions are met.

Core Concepts:
1. Granular Access: A 'Box<T>' might have a 'printDescription()' method for everything, but a 'findMin()' method only when 'T: Comparable'.
2. where Syntax: The constraint is placed after the method's return type (e.g., 'func doSomething() where T: Equatable').
3. Method Visibility: The method exists on all instances of the type, but the compiler will only allow it to be called if the constraint is satisfied.

Example:
    struct Container<T> {
        var item: T
        func compare(to other: T) -> Bool where T: Equatable {
            return item == other
        }
    }

Note: This is more flexible than constraining the whole struct because it doesn't limit the entire type's usefulness.
title: Where Method

cheatsheet:
Generics
- Purpose: Write flexible, reusable functions and types that work with any type.
- Syntax: <T> represents a placeholder for a type.
- Constraints: <T: Equatable> requires the type to implement certain protocols.
- Associated Types: Placeholders used in protocols (associatedtype Item).
- Where Clauses: Add complex requirements to generic parameters (where T: Sequence, T.Element: Equatable).
description: Extend a generic type with specialized behavior that is only available for a specific concrete type (e.g., `T == String`).
hints: Use 'extension Crate where T == String { ... }' to add logic only for strings.
Because of the '== String' constraint, the compiler knows that 'items' contains strings.
The 'firstItem()' method should return the first element of the array or an empty string.
Create a Crate with ["A", "B"] to test the extension.
lesson:
Lesson: Extensions with Specific Constraints
You can extend a generic type and use a 'where' clause to constrain the extension to a specific concrete type (using ==) instead of just a protocol requirement. This is useful for adding behavior that only makes sense for one specific type.

Core Concepts:
1. Concrete Constraints: Using 'where T == String' means the extension only applies when the generic parameter is exactly a String.
2. Targeted Functionality: You can provide helper methods that rely on String-specific APIs (like 'uppercased()') which wouldn't be available on a generic T.
3. Type Specialization: This allows a generic container to act as a specialized container under certain conditions.

Example:
    extension Box where T == Int {
        func increment() -> Int { return content + 1 }
    }

Note: This is a common pattern for adding 'convenience' methods to general-purpose containers.
title: Generic Extension

cheatsheet:
Memory Management (ARC)
- ARC: Automatic Reference Counting manages memory for class instances.
- Strong Reference: The default. Keeps the instance alive as long as the reference exists.
- Weak Reference: Does not keep the instance alive. Automatically becomes 'nil' when the instance is released. Must be 'var' and optional.
- Reference Cycle: Occurs when two instances hold strong references to each other, preventing them from ever being released.
- [weak self]: Used in closure capture lists to avoid reference cycles.
description: Observe how a weak reference automatically becomes `nil` when the object it refers to is deallocated from memory.
hints: Capture the 'forge' instance weakly in the closure capture list: '[weak forge]'.
Because the capture is weak, the closure doesn't keep the forge alive.
When the original reference is set to 'nil', the object is deallocated and the weak reference zeros out.
Use nil coalescing (?? "nil") to print the fallback message.
lesson:
Lesson: Weak Reference Behavior
A weak reference is a reference that doesn't keep the object it refers to alive. Because it doesn't prevent deallocation, the object can be destroyed while the weak reference is still pointing to it.

Core Concepts:
1. Automatic Nullification: When an object is deallocated, ARC automatically sets all weak references pointing to that object to 'nil'.
2. Safety: This prevents 'dangling pointers' (references to memory that has been freed), which would otherwise cause crashes.
3. Variable Requirement: Weak references must always be declared as optional variables ('var') because their value can change to 'nil' at runtime.

Example:
    weak var reference: MyClass? = instance
    instance = nil
    print(reference) // nil

Note: This behavior is unique to classes and is a cornerstone of memory safety in Swift.
title: Weak Release

cheatsheet:
Extensions
- Purpose: Add new functionality to an existing class, structure, enumeration, or protocol type.
- Capabilities:
  - Add computed instance/type properties.
  - Define instance/type methods.
  - Provide new initializers.
  - Define subscripts.
  - Define and use new nested types.
  - Make an existing type conform to a protocol.
- Protocol Extensions: Provide default implementations for protocol requirements.
description: Add a computed property to a struct using an extension to provide derived information about an instance.
hints: Use 'extension Ingot { ... }' to add your property.
The 'isHeavy' property should be a Bool that returns 'true' if weight is 10 or more.
Extensions can add computed properties but NOT stored properties.
Create an Ingot with weight 12 to see 'true' in the output.
lesson:
Lesson: Computed Properties in Extensions
You can extend a type to include computed instance and type properties. This allows you to add derived state or convenience flags to existing models without modifying the original definition.

Core Concepts:
1. Derived State: Properties like 'isHeavy' or 'isValid' that calculate their result from existing data.
2. Stored Property Limitation: Extensions CANNOT add stored properties (properties that need to store actual data in memory). They only support computed properties.
3. self: Use 'self' to access properties of the instance being extended.

Example:
    extension Int {
        var isEven: Bool { return self % 2 == 0 }
    }

Note: This is a great way to keep your data models lean and add behavior only where needed.
title: Extension Computed

cheatsheet:
Error Handling
- Defining Errors: Use an enum that conforms to 'Error'.
- Throwing: Use 'throw MyError.something' inside a function marked 'throws'.
- Handling:
  - do { try function() } catch { ... }
  - try?: Returns an optional, nil if an error was thrown.
  - try!: Disables error propagation; crashes if an error is thrown.
- Rethrows: Used when a function takes a throwing closure and propagates its errors.
description: Implement a higher-order function using the `rethrows` keyword to forward errors from a throwing closure to the caller.
hints: The 'rethrows' keyword indicates that a function only throws if one of its closure parameters throws.
Inside 'run', you must still use 'try' when calling the throwing closure.
If you call 'run' with a non-throwing closure, you don't need to use 'try' at the call site.
Print "Caught" in the catch block to satisfy the output requirement.
lesson:
Lesson: Rethrowing Functions
A function marked with 'rethrows' indicates that it throws an error only if one of its closure parameters throws an error. This is common for functions like 'map', 'filter', and 'forEach' in the standard library.

Core Concepts:
1. Error Forwarding: The function itself doesn't generate errors; it simply passes through any errors thrown by the closure it received.
2. Caller Convenience: If the caller passes a non-throwing closure, the rethrowing function acts as a normal, non-throwing function (no 'try' required).
3. try inside rethrows: Even in a rethrowing function, you must use 'try' when calling the throwing closure in the function body.

Example:
    func execute(_ work: () throws -> Void) rethrows {
        try work()
    }

Note: 'rethrows' is a powerful way to write flexible higher-order functions that stay out of the way when error handling isn't needed.
title: Rethrow
